source_metadata.title_raw: Jimmy Paulding - I-20-010 - February 21, 2020 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 24, 2020

Jimmy Paulding
Councilmember
City of Arroyo Grande
300 E. Branch Street
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-20-010

Dear Mr. Paulding:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Because your request involves general guidance not limited to a specific decision, we are treating it as one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(b)(6)(F). Informal assistance does not provide the requester with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Nothing in this letter should be construed as an evaluation of any conduct which may already have taken place, and any conclusions drawn apply only to prospective actions. (See Regulation 18329(c)(4)(A).)

QUESTIONS

As a councilmember, does the Act prohibit you from taking part in the following decisions:

(1) The City's review and approval of an Accessory Dwelling Unit ("ADU") permit application of a client of your law firm?

(2) Generally applicable City laws and policies regarding ADUs, given that your law firm has clients interested in building ADUs on their residential real properties located within the City?

CONCLUSIONS

(1) Yes. The Act prohibits you from making, participating in making, or using your official position to influence any decisions relating to the City's review and approval of an ADU permit

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless indicated.

File No. I-20-010
Page No. 2

application of a client of your law firm who qualifies as a source of income interest because it is
reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on that source of
income interest. Additionally, we caution that you are prohibited from contacting or appearing
before any city official on behalf of a client to influence a decision regarding an ADU application
during the application process.

(2) To the extent that you assist clients with ADUs, you may be prohibited from taking part
in decisions relating to generally applicable City laws and policies regarding ADUs. However, this
is not a determination we can make absent the specific facts of the decision. Generally, we can only
caution that the Act prohibits you from taking part in such a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision would have a material financial effect on any interest you may have including your
interests in your law firm, as well as the clients of the firm.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember. In your private capacity, you are an
attorney and the sole proprietor of your law firm. You currently have a number of estate-planning
clients interested in building ADUs on their residential real properties located within the City. You
state that you are well positioned to assist your clients through the City's ADU review and approval
process because your education and career have involved city and regional planning. In assisting a
client, you may either informally guide the client through the review and approval process or
formally represent the client through that process.

You provided additional information on January 17, 2020, and January 30, 2020, stating that
your firm's annual gross revenues were approximately $45,000 in 2019 and that decisions relating
to generally applicable laws and policies regarding ADUs, or any client's ADU permit application,
are unlikely to affect your firm's annual gross revenues, assets, or expenses. You note that assisting
clients with ADUs would only make up a small part of your firm's total business.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the
decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) As pertinent to your request,
an official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are set
forth in Section 87103 and include:

• A business interest in any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)), or in which the official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any management position (Section 87103(d)).

• A source of income interest in any source from whom the official receives $500 or more in
value in the 12 months prior to when the decision is made including a pro rata share of
income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater. (Sections 82030 and 87103(c).)

File No. I-20-010
Page No. 3

• An interest in the official's personal finances including those of immediate family members.
(See Section 87103.)²

With respect to the potential decisions relating to ADUs at issue, you have identified
interests in your law firm as both a business interest and source of income, because you hold a
management position with the law firm as its sole proprietor and will receive more than $500
income from the firm in any 12 month period. You have also identified a source of income interest
in any client of your law firm from whom you have received $500 or more value in the 12
months prior to when such a decision is made.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision. An official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision. An interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest including any decision affecting an interest in real property as described
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1) through (6).

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a decision's effect
on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect
on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and
more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Client's ADU Permit

Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an official's source of income interest. Subdivision (a)(1) of that
regulation provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on such an interest is
material if the source is a named party in, or subject of, the decision. A client of your law firm who
qualifies as a source of income interest is a named party in, or subject of, any City decisions relating
to that client's ADU permit application.

Therefore, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to an ADU permit
application of a client who qualifies as source of income interest, regardless of the level of service
you provide the client with respect to the review and approval process for the client's ADU permit
application, because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material
financial effect on your source of income interest in that client.

Additionally, we must caution that taking part in the decision includes making, participating
in making, or attempting to use your official position to influence the decision. (Sections 87100 and
87103.) As provided in Regulation 18704:

² Regulation 18702.5(c) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's interest in his or her personal
finances is not considered separately if the decision affects the official's business interest or real property interest.
Accordingly, we do not analyze any effect on your personal finances further.

File No. I-20-010
Page No. 4

• A public official makes a governmental decision if the official authorizes or directs any
action, votes, appoints a person, obligates, or commits his or her agency to any course of
action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation
18704(a).)

• A public official participates in making a governmental decision if the official provides
information, an opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision
without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b).)

• A public official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if he
or she (1) contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in an agency subject
to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a
decision or (2) contacts or appears before any official in any other government agency for
the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or purports to act within his
or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact. (Regulation
18704(c).)

For any decision before your agency, "using your official position to attempt to influence a
decision" is defined as contacting or appearing before any other officer or employee of your
agency. Because you have stated that you may formally represent clients in ADU matters, we
caution that any representation of clients in these matters will be significantly restricted. Under the
Act you are prohibited from directly taking part in any decision as well as attempting to influence
any City official regarding a decision before the official. Accordingly, you are prohibited from
contacting or appearing before any city official on behalf of a client to influence a decision
regarding an ADU application during the application process, including submitting documents
identifying you as the representative of the applicant.

Generally Applicable City Laws or Policies

To the extent that you assist clients with ADUs, you may be prohibited from taking part in
decisions relating to generally applicable City laws and policies regarding ADU's due to the
potential financial effect on your clients or your law firm. However, this is not a determination we
can make absent the specific facts of a decision. Generally, we can only caution that the Act
prohibits you from taking part in such a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
would have a material financial effect on the clients of the law firm or law firm as outlined below.

Law Firm Clients

Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on an official's source of income interest in an individual is material if:

(A) The decision may affect the individual's income, investments, or other
assets or liabilities by $1,000 or more; or

(B) The official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an
interest in a business entity that will be financially affected under the
materiality standards in Regulation 18702.1; or

File No. I-20-010
Page No. 5

(C) The official knows or has reason to know that the individual has an
interest in real property and;
(i) The property is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision as
defined in Regulations 18701(a) and 18702.2(a)(1) through (6); or

(ii) There is clear and convincing evidence the decision would have a
substantial effect on the property.

With respect to an official's source of income interest in a business entity, Regulation
18702.3(a)(4) provides that a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's
source of income interest in a business entity is material if the business entity "will be financially
affected under the materiality standards of Regulation 18702.1." Under Regulations 18702.1, a
reasonably foreseeable effect is material anytime an interest in a business entity is explicitly
involved in the decision. For a business entity not explicitly involved in the decision, Regulation
18702.1(a)(2) provides that the decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect is material if the
decision may result in an increase or decrease of the business's annual gross revenues, or the value
of the business's assets or liabilities, in an amount equal to or more than $1,000,000, or five percent
of the business's annual gross revenues and the increase or decrease is at least $10,000.

Therefore, the Act would prohibit you from taking part in a City decision relating to a
generally applicable law or policy regarding ADUs if it is reasonably foreseeable that decision
would have a material financial effect on your source of income interest in a client of your law firm
under Regulation 18702.3(a)(2) or Regulation 18702.1.

Interests in Your Law Firm

For your interest in your law firm, Regulation 18702.1(a)(2) also applies to decisions
regarding generally applicable City Laws or Policies in which your firm is not explicitly involved.

You state that your law firm's annual gross revenues were approximately $45,000 in 2019.
Five percent of $45,000 is $2,250. Therefore, the Act would prohibit you from taking part in a City
decision relating to ADUs if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision may result in an increase
or decrease of your law firm's annual gross revenues, or the value of its assets or liabilities, in an
amount equal to or more than $10,000.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

BGL:aja

QUESTION: As a councilmember, does the Act prohibit you from taking part in the following decisions: 
(1) The City's review and approval of an Accessory Dwelling Unit ("ADU") permit 
application of a client of your law firm? 
· (2) Generally applicable City laws and policies regarding ADUs, given that your law 
firm has clients interested in building ADUs on their residential real properties located 
within the City?

CONCLUSION: (1) Yes. The Act prohibits you from making, participating in making, or using your official 
position to influence any decisions relating to the City's review and approval of an ADU permit 
, unless indicated.  
application of a client of your law firm who qualifies as a source of income interest because it is 
reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a material financial effect on that source of 
income interest. Additionally, we caution that you are prohibited from contacting or appearing 
before any city official on behalf of a client to influence a decision regarding an ADU application 
during the application process. 
(2) To the extent that you assist clients with ADUs, you may be prohibited from taking part
